---
title: "Is Singapore Healthcare Adequate for an Ageing Population?"
subtitle: "Statistical Thinking for Data Science"
author: "IS630 Group 1, Team 3"
date: "2025-11-03"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
---

## Project overview

This project assesses whether Singapore's healthcare expenditure and infrastructure have kept pace with growth in the elderly population.

## Analytical approach

The study combines:

- Descriptive statistics.
- Pearson correlation analysis and significance tests.
- Simple linear regression of healthcare expenditure on elderly population share.
- Paired tests comparing elderly-population growth with bed-capacity growth.

## Correlation findings

| Relationship | Correlation | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---|
| Elderly share and health expenditure per capita | 0.96 | Higher ageing is associated with higher per-person spending |
| Elderly share and hospital beds | 0.98-0.99 | Physical capacity broadly tracks ageing |
| Health expenditure and bed occupancy | 0.81 | Added resources are rapidly absorbed by demand |
| Elderly share and community-hospital admissions | 0.89 | Post-acute and rehabilitation needs rise with ageing |
| Elderly share and psychiatric beds | -0.81 | Mental-health capacity appears to lag |

Reported significance tests for elderly share against expenditure and acute, community, and nursing-home beds all have p-values below 0.05.

## Regression result

The simple regression reports:

- $R^2=0.92$: 92% of the observed variation in healthcare expenditure per capita is explained by elderly population share within the analysed sample.
- A positive coefficient for elderly share.
- A p-value below 0.01.

These findings show a strong association between demographic ageing and healthcare expenditure.

## Growth-rate tests

| Comparison | t-statistic | p-value | Reported decision |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Elderly vs acute-bed growth | -5.270 | 0.99996 | Fail to reject the null |
| Elderly vs community-bed growth | 0.823 | 0.21134 | Fail to reject the null |
| Elderly vs nursing-home-bed growth | -0.167 | 0.56539 | Fail to reject the null |

The project finds no statistical evidence that elderly growth has outpaced these bed-capacity categories over the analysis period.

## Interpretation

Singapore's physical healthcare system appears to have expanded broadly in line with population ageing. However, two risks remain:

1. High occupancy suggests that capacity additions are quickly consumed.
2. Psychiatric-bed trends indicate a potential gap in geriatric mental-health services.

## Statistical cautions

- Correlation does not establish causality.
- Variables that trend over time can exhibit strong shared correlations.
- Small time-series samples can limit statistical power.
- Service adequacy depends on staffing, access, quality, waiting times, and case complexity, not beds alone.

Future analysis should test stationarity, lagged effects, demographic and economic confounders, and per-capita or age-standardized service measures.

## Source artifact

- [Group project report](IS630%20Statistical%20Thinking%20Group%20Project.docx)
